O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
02.04.2026 in CRM-M-14148-2026 passed by the High Court
of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh.
3. On  27.04.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“1.  The  petitioner  challenges  the
judgment and order dated 02.04.2026 in
CRMM  No.14148/2026  passed  by  the  High
Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh.
2. We have heard learned counsel for the
petitioner.
3.  Issue  notice,  returnable  on
26.05.2026.
4.  Dasti  service,  in  addition,  is
permitted. Let steps for service be taken
within one (01) week.
5.  Mr.  Samar  Vijay  Singh,  learned
counsel,  accepts  notice  on  behalf  of
1

--- Page 176 ---
respondent no.1/State. Hence, the formal
service of notice on the said respondent
is waived.
6. Learned counsel for the petitioner to
serve a copy of the present petition to
learned  counsel  for  respondent  no.1
forthwith.
7.  Respondent  No.1  shall  file  counter
affidavit  within  three  (03)  weeks.
Rejoinder affidavit thereto, if any, be
filed before the next date of listing.
8.  We  make  it  clear  that  no  further
opportunity  will  be  granted  for  this
purpose.
9.  In  the  notice  itself,  let  it  be
mentioned  that  respondent  no.2  is
required to file the counter affidavit
and  reply  to  the  interlocutory
application(s), if any, before the next
date of listing.
10. In the event of arrest in connection
with  Crime  No.35  of  2026  of  Mullana
Police Station, Ambala District, Haryana,
the petitioner shall be released on bail
on the appropriate terms and conditions
as  may  be  fixed  by  the  Investigating
Officer/Arresting  Officer.  However,  in
the  attending  facts,  one  of  the
conditions shall necessarily be that if
the petitioner possesses a passport, the
2

--- Page 177 ---
same  shall  be  deposited  before  the
Competent  Authority/Court,  till  the
conclusion  of  the  Trial  or  directed
otherwise.
11.  The  petitioner  shall  make  himself
available  before  the  Investigating
Officer on 04.05.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and
on all such dates as he/she/they may be
required.
12.  Needless  to  add,  till  the
investigation  is  not  complete  in  all
respects,  the  petitioner  would  fully
cooperate,  and  if  the  challan/charge
sheet is filed, he would maintain good
conduct and not attempt to influence any
of the witnesses in any manner till the
completion of the trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 27.04.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
3

--- Page 178 ---
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment and order dated 02.04.2026 is
set aside.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.9751/2026]
RAVISHANKAR YADAV                  Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH             Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The  appellant  seeks  regular  bail  in
connection with FIR No.218/2025 dated 24.06.2025,
registered  at  Police  Station  -  Baikunthpur,
District - Korea, Chhattisgarh for the offences
punishable under Sections 111, 317(2), 317(4) and
317(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
1

--- Page 191 ---
The  case  of  the  prosecution  against  the
appellant is that he facilitated the commission of
the offence by allowing main accused person(s) to
use the bank account opened in his name, as a mule
account for depositing a sum of Rs. 4,58,826/-
approximately. We have also been informed that
similarly placed co-accused person(s) have been
granted the benefit of bail.
Considering  the  above  circumstances  along
with the period of incarceration already undergone
by the appellant, we are inclined to grant regular
bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order stands set
aside and the appellant is granted bail on the
terms and conditions to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court.
2

--- Page 192 ---
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
...............….J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
NEW DELHI;
JULY 13, 2026.

Police Station Pasgawan, District-Kheri, for the
offence punishable under Section 304 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860.
1

--- Page 196 ---
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
On a query raised, learned counsel appearing
for the State acknowledged the fact that the
similarly placed co-accused has been granted bail.
However, a distinction is sought to be made on the
premise that the earlier rejection of bail qua the
appellant was not taken into consideration while
granting bail to the co-accused, which, in our
considered  view,  cannot  be  the  basis  to
differentiate the case of the appellant.  
Admittedly,  the  appellant  has  been  under
incarceration for a period of three years and five
months.  We also take note of the charge framed
against the appellant and the specific overt act
attributed to him. Suffice it is to state that the
appellant is entitled to bail.
In such view of the matter, we are inclined
to grant bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order is set aside
2

--- Page 197 ---
and the appellant is granted bail on terms and
conditions to the satisfaction of the concerned
Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL  NO.                   OF 2026
(@ SLP(CRL.) No. 1982/2026)
  SHIV KUMAR SAHU                 APPELLANT(S)
                     VERSUS
THE STATE OF JHARKHAND & ANR.   RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. We have heard the learned counsel appearing for
the  appellant  and  the  learned  Additional
Solicitor General and learned counsel appearing
for the respondents. 
3. The appellant has been arraigned as an accused
and seeks regular bail in connection with NIA
FIR  RC-04/2023/NIA/RNC  dated  11.10.2023,
registered at Police Station - NIA, Ranchi for
the offences punishable under Sections 120-B and
384 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and Sections
13,  17,  18,  18B  and  20  of  the  Unlawful
1  SLP(Crl.) No.1982/2026

--- Page 211 ---
Activities (Prevention) Act, 1967.
4. The  case  of  the  prosecution  against  the
appellant  is  that  he  facilitated  the  other
accused person(s) in committing the offence. 
5. Though, the learned counsel appearing for the
appellant has argued on merits, we find that
there are adequate materials available on record
even insofar as the extent of charging the
appellant is concerned. 
6. However, we find that a period of more than two
years has lapsed and only eight out of the total
196 prosecution witnesses have been examined so
far. We also have taken into consideration the
specific allegation against appellant, which in
our consideration also warrants the grant of
regular bail. Suffice it is to state, that it
will take a substantial number of years to
conclude the trial. 
7. Considering the facts and circumstances of the
case, we are inclined to set aside the impugned
order passed by the High Court and grant bail to
the appellant.
8. Accordingly,  the  impugned  order  stands  set
aside,  and  the  appellant  is  granted  bail,
2  SLP(Crl.) No.1982/2026

--- Page 212 ---
subject to the terms and conditions that may be
imposed by the concerned Trial Court.
9. The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
10. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

  [M.M. SUNDRESH]

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.831/2026]
JASPREET SINGH                  Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA                  Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The  appellant  apprehends  his  arrest  in
connection with FIR No.369/2024 dated 16.11.2024,
registered at Police Station Faridabad Central,
District-Faridabad  for  the  offences  punishable
under Sections 420, 120-B of the Indian Penal
Code, 1860 and Sections 3, 4 and 5 of the Haryana
Protection of Interest of Depositors in Financial
Establishment Act, 2013.
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
1

--- Page 216 ---
Seeking to overturn the order rejecting his
application for anticipatory bail, the appellant
has come before us.  
Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
submits that he was merely a nominee Director of
the company and had already resigned from the post
in  the  year  2021  itself.  The  FIR  has  been
registered only in the year 2024, and in any case,
the co-accused have been granted regular bail.
Furthermore, he has been cooperating with the
investigation.
Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the  State
submits  that  at  the  time  of  the  alleged
occurrence, the appellant was functioning as the
Director and dealt with the relevant records.
Though the allegations are quite serious, the
foundational facts are not in dispute which are to
the effect that the appellant before us was a non-
executive Director and had resigned from the job
in the year 2021. Therefore, there is a marked
2

--- Page 217 ---
difference between the case of the appellant as
against the case of the other co-accused who have
been granted regular bail. Furthermore, he has
joined the investigation.
Considering the above, we are inclined to
grant anticipatory bail to the appellant.
Accordingly, the impugned order is set aside
and the appellant is granted anticipatory bail on
terms and conditions to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

[M.M. SUNDRESH]

[PRASANNA B. VARALE]

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.     /2026]
   [@ DIARY NO.73806/2025]
MD. ASIF RAZA @ ASHIF RAZA
@ HAFIZ JI              Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.     Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Delay condoned.
Leave granted.
The  appellant  apprehends  his  arrest  in
connection  with  FIR  No.51360011250139  dated
15.03.2025, registered at Police Station-Akbarpur,
District  –  Nawada,  Bihar  for  the  offences
punishable under Sections 126(2), 115(2), 351(2)
109 and 85 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
and Sections 3 and 4 of the Dowry Prohibition
Act,1961.
1

--- Page 221 ---
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Taking note of the submissions made by the
learned counsel appearing for the appellant, we
had earlier directed the learned counsel appearing
for  the  respondent  -  State  to  get  adequate
instructions regarding registration of the case
FIR by the deceased, especially when the FIR was
allegedly registered in her name at a place which
was approximately 150 kms away from the place of
her demise.
Admittedly, the appellant is one who was
attending the deceased during her treatment and
brought her to the hospital, as she was suffering
from  Stage  IV  Ovarian  Cancer.  We  have  been
informed at the Bar that the FIR has been wrongly
registered as the complaint itself was given by
the sister of the deceased, i.e., the respondent
no. 2. Even the said stand cannot be countenanced,
as the FIR proceeds on a premise as if the FIR was
registered by the deceased herself. 
2

--- Page 222 ---
Be that as it may, we are not inclined to go into
the other issues involved, as the facts governing
itself are sufficient to depict that the appellant
is entitled for the grant of anticipatory bail.
Accordingly, the impugned order passed by the
High Court stands set aside and the appellant is
granted  anticipatory  bail  on  the  terms  and
conditions to the satisfaction of the concerned
Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(s).         OF 2026
(arising out of SLP (Crl.) No(s).7682/2026)
BIJU PJ                                            APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
THE STATE OF KERALA                                RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
03.02.2026 in Bail Application No.426/2026 passed by the
High Court of Kerala at Ernakulam.
3. On  24.04.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“1.  The  petitioner  challenges  the
judgment and order dated 03-02-2026 in BA
No.426/2026 titled “Biju P.J. vs. State
of Kerala“ passed by the High Court of
Kerala at Ernakulam. 
2. Delay condoned.
3.  Issue  notice,  returnable  on
25.05.2026.
4.  Mr.  Harshad  V.Hameed,  learned
Advocate-on-Record,  appears  and  accepts
notice on behalf of the State of Kerala.
Hence, the formal service of notice on
the respondent-State is waived.
5. Let counter affidavit be filed within
two weeks. Rejoinder thereto, if any, be
filed within one week thereafter.
6. In the event of arrest in connection
with  FIR  No.1436/2025  dated  17.12.2025
registered  at  P.S.  Pampady,
DistrictKottayam, the petitioner shall be
released  on  bail  by  the
1

--- Page 238 ---
Investigating/Arresting  Officer  on  such
terms and conditions as imposed and found
to be just, fair and reasonable. However,
in  the  attending  facts,  one  of  the
conditions shall necessarily be that if
the petitioner possesses a Passport, the
same  shall  be  deposited  before  the
Competent  Authority/Court,  until  the
conclusion of the Trial.
7.  The  petitioner  shall  make  himself
available  before  the  Investigating
Officer on 04.05.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and
on all such dates as he may be required.
8. Needless to add, the petitioner would
maintain good conduct and not attempt to
influence any of the witnesses, in any
manner,  till  the  completion  of  the
trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 24.04.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
2

--- Page 239 ---
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment and order dated 03.02.2026 is
set aside.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
..................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)
..................J.
(AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH)

--- Page 246 ---

released on bail on the appropriate terms
and conditions as may be fixed by the
Investigating  Officer/Arresting Officer.

the conditions shall necessarily be that
if the petitioner possesses a passport,
the same shall be deposited before the
Competent  Authority/Court,  till  the
conclusion  of  the  Trial  or  directed
otherwise.

available  before  the  Investigating

sheet is filed, he would maintain good
conduct and not attempt to influence any
of the witnesses in any manner till the
completion of the trial.”

also perused the materials available on record.

complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the

1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated

Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh.
3. On  07.05.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“1.  The  petitioner  challenges  the
judgment and order dated 30.03.2026 in
CRMM No.9638/2026 (O&M) titled “Hardial
Singh vs. State of Punjab“ passed by the
High  Court  of  Punjab  &  Haryana  at

2.  Issue  notice,  returnable  on
13.07.2026.
3. Ms. Nupur Kumar, learned Advocate-on-

the learned counsel for the r espondent-

two weeks. Rejoinder thereto, if any, be

1

--- Page 250 ---
6. In the event of arrest in connection
with F.I.R. No.9/2026 dated 09.01.2026,

4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.

which it was allegedly committed.

dated 07.05.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the

--- Page 251 ---
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary

Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on

ITEM NO.6               COURT NO.6               SECTION II-E
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No(s).12152/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 18-04-2026
in MCRLC No. 17308/2026 passed by the High Court of Madhya Pradesh
at Gwalior]
NEELESH RAJPUT & ANR.                              Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH                            Respondent(s)
IA No. 190700/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 190701/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Pradeep Aggarwal, Adv.
                   Mr. Lal Pratap Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Umesh Pratap Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Arjun Aggarwal, AOR
                   Mr. Vishal Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Aman Kumar, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Surjeet Singh, Govt. Adv. 
    Mr. Yashraj Singh Bundela, AOR
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. It appears from the materials on record that the petitioners
before us came to be arrested in connection with Crime No.818/2024,
registered with Vidisha Rural Police Station for the offence
punishable under Sections 318(1), 336, 338, 340, 111(1), 111(3),
1

--- Page 254 ---
SLP (Crl.) No.12152/2026
111(4), 61(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, respectively
(for short, “the BNS, 2023”) and for the offence punishable under
the  provisions  of  Madhya  Pradesh  Protection  of  Depositors’
Interests Act, 2000 and the Banning of Unregulated Deposit Schemes
(Prohibition) Act, 2019, respectively.
2. It is the case of the petitioners that they were produced
before  the  Second  Additional  Sessions  Judge,  Vidisha,  Madhya
Pradesh on 27.01.2025 in connection with the FIR referred to above.
3. The learned counsel invited our attention to the order passed
by the Second Additional Sessions Judge, Vidisha, Madhya Pradesh
dated 27.01.2025. The order reads thus:-
"The Presiding Officer is on training. 
On behalf of the State, Mr. Dhoop Singh Singh
Tomar, Additional Public Prosecutor, is present. 
Accused Mahendra Sarathe is lodged in jail; Mr.
K.G. Maheshwari appears on his behalf. 
Accused Nilesh, Brajesh, and Gorelal are present
through production warrant. 
Put up as before on 06.02.2025.”
4. The  petitioners  fairly  pointed  out  that  when  they  were
produced on 27.01.2025, they were already in judicial custody in
connection with some another case.
5. According to the petitioners, the period of 90 days expired on
26.04.2025. Till 26.04.2025, no chargesheet was filed in connection
with  FIR  No.818/2024.  In  such  circumstances,  the  petitioners
preferred an application before the Sessions Court under Section
167(2) of the Cr.P.C and prayed for default bail. The application
came to be rejected by the Sessions Court vide order dated
2

--- Page 255 ---
SLP (Crl.) No.12152/2026
29.04.2025, essentially on the ground that there is nothing to
indicate that on 27.01.2025, the two petitioners were actually
produced before the Sessions Court after their arrest. The relevant
findings recorded by the learned Sessions Judge reads thus:-
“In the present case, since the accused persons
were already lodged in jail in another case, the date
fixed for their production through production-warrant
was 27.01.2025. According to the police, report, the
accused persons did not appear before the Court on the
fixed date, 27.01.2025. On this point, the defence
counsel submitted that the order sheet for 27.01.2025
records  that  the  accused  were  produced  through
production  warrant.  However,  upon  examination  of
27.01.2025, it is evident that the Presiding Officer was
on training on that date, and the case record was placed
before the supervising officer; moreover, there are no
signatures on the order sheet regarding the presence of
the accused on that date. In this connection, a report
was also obtained from the District Jail, Vidisha, and
that report also clarifies that the accused persons were
not produced before the Court on production warrant on
27.01.2025.”
6. Being dissatisfied with the order passed by the learned
Sessions Judge declining to release the petitioners on default
bail, went before the High Court. The High Court also declined to
interfere. In such circumstances, the petitioners are here before
us with the present petition.
7. The short point for our consideration is whether the period of
90 days should be counted from 27.01.2025, i.e., the date of the
petitioners’ production before the Sessions Court. If there is
anything to indicate that in fact the petitioners were actually
produced  before  the  Sessions  Court  on  27.01.2025,  then,
3

--- Page 256 ---
SLP (Crl.) No.12152/2026
undoubtedly, the period of 90 days is to be counted from 27.01.2025
and the 90 th day came to an end on 26.04.2025. The petitioners
preferred the application on 28.04.2025, the chargesheet came to be
filed on 05.05.2025. The application for default bail came to be
rejected indisputably before the chargesheet was filed.
8. We want to ascertain from the Trial Court through the High
Court the correct position. We want to know whether, in fact, there
was actual production of the two petitioners before the concerned
Sessions Court on 27.01.2025 or not.
9. We would also like to know the basis for the finding recorded
by the Sessions Court referred to above that they were actually not
produced. We say so in view of the order passed by the Sessions
Court dated 27.01.2025.
10. We direct the Registrar General, High Court of Madhya Pradesh
to undertake an appropriate inquiry in this regard and file the
report before us as regards the exact position, more particularly,
the exact date on which the petitioners were produced before the
Court concerned in connection with FIR No.818/2024. Let the report
be forwarded within a period of two weeks from today.
11. Registry shall forward one copy of this order to the Registrar
General, High Court of Madhya Pradesh today itself along with
entire papers of the Special Leave Petition.
12. List the Special Leave Petition on 28.07.2026.
(HARPREET KAUR)                                 (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 257 ---

Crl. Appeal No.2538/2026  1/4

        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made 
the following
                     O R D E R
1. The appellant along with other persons stood
trial for offences under Sections 302, 364, 365,
201 and Section 120B of the Indian Penal Code,
1860 (IPC). The appellant who was the 6 th accused,
along with other accused were convicted by the
Sessions Court which conviction stood confirmed in
an  appeal  filed  before  the  High  Court.  The
appellant inter alia has been imposed with a life
sentence.
2. The  appellant  is  before  this  Court
challenging  the  conviction  and  specifically
pointing out that he has been incarcerated for
more than 15 years.
3. We heard Dr. Anindita Pujari, learned Senior
Counsel  appearing  for  the  appellant  and  Mr.
Rudraditya Khare, learned D.A.G. appearing for the
respondent State. 
4. The learned Senior Counsel for the appellant
submits that there is every chance of success in
Crl. Appeal No.2538/2026  2/4

--- Page 259 ---
the appeal and despite concurrent findings, the
appellant has a good case for acquittal. 
5. The  deceased  was  allegedly  abducted  on
17.11.2007 and his body was detected after 10 days
on  27.11.2007.  An  FIR  with  respect  to  the
abduction is said to have been registered on the
same day after about an hour of the incident. We
do  not  see  any  material  regarding  the
investigation carried out therein as placed by the
prosecution before the trial court. It is also
pertinent that the body was recovered on the
disclosure statement of a co-accused, whose appeal
was withdrawn before the High Court. 
6. On a prima facie consideration, we are of the
opinion that the sentence of the appellant can be
suspended pending appeal, especially considering
the long incarceration and the appellant released
for the time being. 
7. The sentence imposed on the appellant is
suspended and he is ordered to be released on
bail, subject to terms and conditions the trial
Crl. Appeal No.2538/2026  3/4

--- Page 260 ---
court may deem fit to impose.
8. Registry  shall  call  for  the  records  and
proceedings of the trial court in both forms,
physical and digital. The digital record shall be
provided  to  both  the  learned  counsel  at  the
earliest.
9. Notify the main appeal for final hearing in
February, 2027.
(HARPREET KAUR)                  (POOJA SHARMA)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.            /2026
[@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.8118/2026]
AYUSH KETULBHAI PANCHAL              APPELLANT(S)
                      VERSUS
STATE OF GUJARAT                     RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order  dated
11.03.2026 whereby the High Court of Gujarat has
rejected the regular bail prayer of the appellant
in connection with First Information Report being
C.R. No.11204041250893/2025.
4. The submission of the learned counsel for the
appellant is that the offences for which the
appellant  is  being  tried  are  all  Magistrate
triable offences and the appellant, by now, has
suffered incarceration of nearly 10 months.  It
has also been submitted that the appellant has no
previous criminal antecedents.
5. The learned counsel for the State has opposed
the prayer for bail by submitting that there
Crl. @ SLP (Crl.) No.8118/2026 1

--- Page 262 ---
appears to be recovery of two gold bangles from
the appellant.
6. Be that as it may, having regard to the
nature of the offences as also that they are
triable by a Court of Magistrate and the appellant
has suffered pending trial incarceration of nearly
10 months, we are of the view that the appellant
is entitled to be released on bail, at this stage.
7. Consequently, the appeal, is allowed.  The
impugned order passed by the High Court rejecting
the bail prayer of the appellant stands set aside.
8. The appellant shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as the trial court may
deem fit to impose in the facts and circumstances
of the case.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order  dated
27.02.2026 passed by the High Court of Gujarat at
Ahmedabad rejecting the regular bail prayer of the
appellant in connection with First Information
Report  being  C.R.  No.112160222200063  of  2022
registered  with  Kalol  Taluka  Police  Station,
Gandhi Nagar.
4. At the outset, the learned counsel for the
appellant submits that though appellant is made an
accused,  he  was  neither  named  in  the  First
Information  Report  nor  a  Test  Identification
Parade was carried out.  There is no incriminating
recovery from the appellant and all co-accused
other than the appellant have been admitted to
bail.   Appellant’s  bail  prayer  was  rejected
primarily on the ground that he was absconding
since  the  lodging  of  the  FIR  and  could  be
apprehended after two years.  It is the case of
SLP (Crl.) No.7709/2026                                                                        1

--- Page 266 ---
the appellant that by now he has already suffered
incarceration for a period of about two years and,
therefore, at this stage the appellant is entitled
to be released on bail.
5. The learned counsel for the State has opposed
the prayer for bail but could not dispute the fact
that there was no incriminating recovery from the
appellant; that other accused are on bail; and
that the appellant has suffered pending trial
incarceration of about two years.
6. Having  regard  to  the  above,  without
expressing any opinion on the merits of the case,
we are of the view that the appellant is entitled
to be released on bail, pending trial.
7. The  appeal  is,  accordingly,  allowed.  The
order passed by the High Court rejecting the bail
prayer of the appellant is set aside.
8. The appellant shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as the trial court may
deem fit to impose in the facts and circumstances
of the case.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.            /2026
[@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.8035/2026]
SHIVA THAKUR @ MOHIT SINH VIJAYSINH           Appellant(s)
                          VERSUS
THE STATE OF GUJARAT     Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted. 
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties. 
3. This  appeal  arises  from  an  order  dated
11.02.2026  passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Gujarat
rejecting  the  anticipatory  bail  prayer  of  the
appellant  in  connection  with  FIR  being  C.R.No.
11210069250586,  registered  at  P.S.  Althan,  Surat,
Gujarat.
4. The submission of the learned counsel for the
appellant is that the thrust of the allegations is
against co-accused Vivek and Alka Thakur. Besides, the
allegations are in respect of money being advanced for
making a movie which could not be ultimately made.
1

--- Page 278 ---
Therefore, according to the appellant, the allegations
are in respect of a civil dispute.
5. Having regard to the aforesaid submissions, on
04.05.2026, we had passed an interim order which is
extracted below:
“1.  Issue  notice,  returnable  in  six
weeks.
2. Notice on behalf of the respondent has
been accepted by Ms Swati Ghildiyal, AOR.
3. In the meantime, it is provided that
in the event the Petitioner is arrested
in  connection  with  C.R.  No.
11210069250586/2025, registered at P.S.
Althan,  District  Surat,  he  shall  be
released  on  a  personal  bond  of  Rs.
25,000/- subject to an undertaking that
he shall cooperate in the investigation
and  make  himself  available  for
interrogation as and when required by the
investigating  agency.  He  shall  also
submit an undertaking that he will not
threaten  the  witnesses  or  tamper  the
evidence.”
6. The learned counsel for the State does not dispute
that the appellant has co-operated in the investigation
thus far.
7. Having regard to the above, we deem it appropriate to
dispose of the appeal by making the interim order absolute
subject to the following two conditions.
(A) The  appellant  shall  co-operate  in  the
investigation  and  make  himself  available  for
2

--- Page 279 ---
interrogation as and when required by the investigating
agency; and
(B) Within three weeks from today, he shall
submit bail bonds to the satisfaction of the Trial
Court concerned along with an undertaking that he shall
not threaten the witnesses or tamper the evidence.
8. The appeal is disposed of in the above terms.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
……………………………………………………J
                  [MANOJ MISRA]
……………………………………………………J
                             [VIJAY BISHNOI]
New Delhi
JULY 13, 2026

--- Page 281 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.   3127   OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.9579/2026)
AYUB         APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF ODISHA          RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1.  We have heard learned counsel for the appellant and
learned counsel for the respondent-State of Odisha.
2. Leave granted.
3. At the outset, learned counsel for the respondent-
State of Odisha sought for time by circulating a letter
for filing counter-affidavit. The said prayer has been
objected to by learned counsel for the appellant.
4. This is a matter relating to bail.
5. Notice in this case was issued on 14.05.2026. On
27.05.2026, on the request of learned counsel for the
respondent-State of Odisha, two weeks’ time was granted to
seeks  instructions  and  for  filing  counter  affidavit,
making it clear that no more time would be allowed.
6. In view thereof, we do not find any justification for
1

--- Page 282 ---
granting further time to the respondent-State of Odisha to
file counter affidavit and instead proceed to hear the
matter on merit.
7. Learned counsel for the appellant, referring to the
relevant portion of the charge sheet, submits that no
recovery was effected from the appellant. Appellant is in
custody since 17.06.2023. Though evidence of 07 out of 16
witnesses have been recorded, two of the crucial witnesses
have turned hostile. As of today, there is no adverse
material against the appellant. In such circumstances, the
continuous detention of the appellant would not be just
and proper.
8. Learned counsel for the respondent-State of Odisha,
on the other hand, has referred to Paragraph 3.1 of the
impugned order and submits that appellant has a criminal
antecedent of similar nature. On being granted bail in the
said case, appellant has committed the present offence.
9. After hearing learned counsel for the parties and on
due  consideration,  more  particularly  the  fact  that
appellant is in custody since 17.06.2023 and no recovery
of the contraband being made from him with two of the
prosecution witnesses turning hostile, we are of the view
that a case for grant of bail to the appellant is made
out.
10. Considering the above, we direct that appellant shall
be produced before the concerned Special Judge within a
maximum  period  of  seven  days  from  today  who  shall
2

--- Page 283 ---
thereafter release the appellant on bail on such terms and
conditions as may be deemed appropriate. We, however, put
the appellant on notice that he shall co-operate with the
ongoing trial and in case the appellant indulges in
similar kind of activities, the respondent-State of Odisha
would be at liberty to file application for cancellation
of bail of the appellant.
11. Accordingly, the Criminal Appeal is allowed.
12. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
…………………………………………………...J.
      
   [UJJAL BHUYAN]
…………………………………………………...J.
     [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
13th JULY, 2026.
AK
3

--- Page 284 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.   3128   OF 2026
(@ SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRIMINAL) NO.6375/2026)
SHAHID @ LABADU        APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA          RESPONDENT(S)
ORDER
1.  We have heard learned counsel for the appellant and
learned counsel for the respondent-State of Haryana.
2. Leave granted.
3. This appeal is directed against the order dated
18.02.2026 passed by the High Court of Punjab and Haryana
(briefly the ‘High Court’ hereinafter), rejecting the
application of the appellant for suspension of sentence
during pendency of the Criminal Appeal before the High
Court, further directing the Registry of the High Court to
list the Criminal Appeal for hearing within a period of
one year.
4. It is stated that appellant has been convicted by the
Trial Court for committing offences under Sections 379,
392 and 397 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (in short
1

--- Page 286 ---
‘IPC’)  and  Sections  25/54/59  of  Arms  Act,  1959  and
sentenced to undergo Rigorous Imprisonment (R.I.) for 10
years and to pay fine of Rs.2,000/-, with a default
stipulation.
5. Appellant’s appeal against conviction being CRA-S-
1765-SB-2014 is pending before the High Court.
6. Learned counsel for the appellant submits that out of
10 years’ sentence, appellant has already undergone more
than 04 years 05 months of actual incarceration.
7. On a query by the Court, learned counsel for the
respondent-State of Haryana submits that considering the
pendency of criminal appeals before the High Court, the
hearing of the related appeal may take some time.
8. This Court vide order dated 17.04.2026 had issued
notice.
9. After hearing learned counsel for the parties and
considering the materials on record, we are of the view
that notwithstanding the direction of the High Court to
the Registry to list the Criminal Appeal for hearing
within a period of one year and having regard to the
sentence already undergone by the appellant, it would meet
the ends in the justice if the sentence is suspended and
the appellant is released on bail during pendency of the
appeal before the High Court.
10. Accordingly, appellant shall be produced before the
Trial Court within a maximum period of seven days from
today who shall thereafter enlarge the appellant on bail
2

--- Page 287 ---
on such terms and conditions as may be deemed appropriate.
11. The Criminal Appeal is disposed of.
12 Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
…………………………………………………...J.
      
   [UJJAL BHUYAN]
…………………………………………………...J.
     [ATUL S. CHANDURKAR]
NEW DELHI;
13th JULY, 2026.
AK
3

176/2012, W.P.(C) No. 320/2012 W.P.(C) No. 383/2012 and   W.P.(C) No.  
110/2014
1. Learned counsel for the parties are ad idem on the
issue that the issue involved in the Writ Petition(s)
concerning charging of sales tax / value added tax on
supply and installation of lifts and air-conditioners have
been dealt with by this Court in  Kone Elevator India
3

--- Page 296 ---
Private Ltd. vs. State of Tamil Nadu 1. Therefore, the
assessment  or  re-assessment  in  the  matter  has  to  be
undertaken in view of law laid down in the matter of Kone
Elevator (supra).
2. In  view  of  the  above,  we  dispose  of  the  Writ
Petition(s) reserving liberty in favour of the Assessing
Officer to undertake the fresh assessment or re-assessment
as the case may be, keeping in view the law laid down by
this Court in Kone Elevator (supra).
(MINI)                            (AKSHAY KUMAR BHORIA)

ITEM NO.33               COURT NO.12               SECTION XIV
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  22724/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 22-06-2026
in CMAPPL No. 39115/2026 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New
Delhi]
URVASHI SENGAR                                     Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ANR.                              Respondent(s)
IA No. 189344/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 10-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SHREE CHANDRASHEKHAR
[PARTIAL COURT WORKING DAYS BENCH]
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Avinash K. Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Ashutosh Nagar, AOR
                   Mr. Uditanshu Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Palkriti N. Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. Navtej Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Praveen Kumar, Adv.
                   Ms. Jahanvi Raj, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Anil Kaushik, ASG
Mr. Syed Abdul Haseeb, Adv.
Mr. Sai Shashank, Adv.
Mr. Pranjal Singh, Adv.
Mr. M. Vishakamurthy, AOR
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. This petition seeks to impugn an interim order
passed by the High Court dated 22.06.2026 whereby
the High Court had stayed the interim order passed
1

--- Page 306 ---
by  the  Central  Administrative  Tribunal  dated
27.05.2026 passed in Original Application No.1855
of  2026,  whereby  the  Tribunal  directed  the
respondents to permit the petitioner to undergo
training.
2. On 08.07.2026, after hearing the learned counsel
for the parties we had passed the following order:
“1.  The  submission  of  the  learned
counsel for the petitioner is that
Office Memorandum (“O.M.”) issued by
the Ministry of Home Affairs which is
in force since 23.08.1993 is to be
construed as one to protect lady I.P.S
probationers and not as one which dis-
entitles them to undertake training
even if they are fit to undertake
training. 
2. It is the case of the petitioner
that  she  delivered  a  child  on
20.09.2025  and  volunteered  for  the
requisite  training  which  were  to
commence on 22.06.2026 but, because of
this O.M. which provides for a year’s
hiatus in training post-delivery, the
Department  did  not  permit  her  to
undertake training. Accordingly, the
said O.M. was challenged before the
Central  Administrative  Tribunal
(“Tribunal”)  which,  by  a  detailed
order,  permitted  the  petitioner  to
participate in the Phase-II training
which were to commence on 22.06.2026
subject  to  fulfillment  of  the
requisite  undertakings,  formalities,
medical  requirements,  etc.  While
passing the said interim order, the
Tribunal  had  also  noticed  the
submissions  made  on  behalf  of  the
petitioner  that  earlier  Ms  Anupama
James and Ms Arti Singh were given
exemption from the rigors of the O.M.
3. Issue notice.
4. Notice on behalf of the respondent
2

--- Page 307 ---
has  been  accepted  by  Dr  M.
Vishakhamurthy, AOR.
5. As it appears from the impugned
order that training were to commence
from  22.06.2026,  we  deem  it
appropriate to require the learned ASG
to seek instructions whether at this
stage  the  petitioner  can  join  the
training.
6. Put up on 10.07.2026.”
3. Pursuant to the above order, Mr. Anil Kaushik,
learned ASG has obtained instructions regarding the
query put in our order dated 08.07.2026. As per his
instructions, the entire training is of nine weeks.  By
now, three weeks of training is over. In first three
weeks, that is by today, number of exercises have
already been carried out. He submits that it may not be
feasible to carry out separate training program for the
petitioner as it includes lecture programs also.  He,
therefore, submits that if the petitioner is permitted
to undergo training at this stage, her training would
be incomplete. Besides, there would be a shortfall in
the attendance as is required in the academy.
4. Having regard to the above, we are of the view that
the interim order passed by the Central Administrative
Tribunal, which has been stayed by the impugned order
passed by the High Court, has outlived its utility.
Therefore, no useful purpose would be served even if we
set aside the impugned order of the High Court.
3

--- Page 308 ---
5. In such circumstances, we deem it appropriate to
dispose of this petition by observing that the Tribunal
shall proceed with the original application filed by
the petitioner and decide the same expeditiously on its
own merits without being prejudiced by any observations
in the impugned order as well as this order.  However,
the Tribunal shall not insist on enforcement of the
interim order which has outlived its utility.
6. At this stage, the learned ASG states that in the
light of this order, he undertakes to withdraw the writ
petition filed before the High Court so that the matter
could be contested before the Tribunal on merits.
7. The said undertaking is accepted.
8. The petition is disposed of in the aforesaid terms.
   (KAVITA PAHUJA)                              (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
4

--- Page 309 ---
ITEM NO.32               COURT NO.1               SECTION X
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Writ Petition(s)(Criminal)  No(s).  364/2025
RAJIYA ALI                                         Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA & ORS.                              Respondent(s)
(IA No. 314584/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T. and IA No.
225820/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
WITH
W.P.(Crl.) No. 9/2026 (X)
(IA No. 9849/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.)
 
Date : 13-07-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE THE CHIEF JUSTICE
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE JOYMALYA BAGCHI
         HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE V. MOHANA
For Petitioner(s) : M/S. Varun Thakur & Associates, AOR
Mr. Prashant Bhushan, Adv.
Mr. Manish Pratap Singh, Adv.
Mr. Anurag Tiwari, Adv.
Mr. Mohan Kumar, AOR
Mr. Narendra Hooda, Sr. Adv.
Mr. Manish Pratap Singh Chauhan, Adv.
Mr. Mohan Kumar, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Rajkumar Bhaskar Thackre, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Rohit Khare, Adv.
                   Mr. Aaditya Shankar Dixit, Adv.
                   Mr. Ishaan Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Vijay Awana, Adv.
                   Mr. S.N. Terdal(aor), Adv.
                   Mr. Aaditya Pandey, Adv.
                   Mr. Mukesh Kumar Maroria, AOR
                   
                   Mr. S.V. Raju, A.S.G.
                   Mr. Sridhar Potaraju, A.A.G.
                   Mr. D.S. Parmar, A.A.G.
                   Mr. Harmeet Singh Ruprah, AOR
                   Mr. Saurabh Singh, Adv.
1

--- Page 310 ---
                                      
                   Ms. Vibha Datta Makhija, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Vibha Datta Makhija, Adv.
                   Mr. Amit Dwivedi, AOR
                   Mr. Padmesh Mishra, Adv.
                   Ms. Rohini Narayanan, Adv.
                   Ms. Lv Sri Nehaol, Adv.
                   Mr. Aman Verma, Adv.
                                      
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. These writ petitions under Article 32 of the Constitution of
India have been filed by Rajiya Ali, widow of Salman Khan and
Kunwar Vikram Singh @ Nati Raja seeking transfer of investigation
of FIR Nos.321/2023 and 322/2023 dated 17.11.2023 and 19.11.2023
registered at P.S. Khajuraho, District Chhatarpur (Madhya Pradesh)
to Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) or an independent Special
Investigation Team (SIT).
2. The petitioner’s husband (Salman Khan) was working as a Driver
with one Kunwar Vikram Singh @ Nati Raja, the then MLA and was a
contesting candidate of the Congress party.  It is alleged that one
Arvind  Pateria,  a  candidate  of  the  BJP,  and  his  associates
deliberately  ran  a  vehicle  over  Salman  Khan  during  election
campaigning,  as  a  result  of  which  he  suffered  injuries  and
eventually died an unnatural death. This incident led to the
registration of the subject FIRs. The petitioner, however, alleges
that  due  to  alleged  political  influence,  there  was  no  fair
investigation in the matter.  During the course of the hearing, it
is pointed out that there are four eyewitnesses to the occurrence
besides the complainant, and their version has not been recorded by
the investigating authority. These witnesses are also stated to
have given their affidavits to the police.
2

--- Page 311 ---
3. On the other hand, Mr. S.V. Raju, learned Additional Solicitor
General of India representing the State of Madhya Pradesh, has
strongly  refuted  the  allegations  as,  according  to  him,  the
investigation  has  been  conducted  fairly,  independently  and
impartially. He submits that the investigation is near completion.
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties.  Having regard
to the nature of allegations and counter-allegations, it seems to
us, in the interest of justice and fair play, that the subject FIRs
should be investigated by a team of senior officers.
5. We, consequently, direct the Director General of Police,
Madhya Pradesh, to constitute an SIT, comprising three officers,
one in the rank of Senior Superintendent of Police (IPS Cadre) and
two officers, not below the rank of Deputy Superintendent of Police
and Inspector of Police.  The officer heading the SIT shall be from
the IPS cadre of Madhya Pradesh, but he should not belong to the
State of Madhya Pradesh.  The officers to be included in the SIT
shall be outside the jurisdiction of Chhatarpur district. The SIT
shall be constituted within two days, and shall then take over the
entire record from the current investigating authority and shall
proceed afresh in accordance with law.
6. It goes without saying that the affidavits/statements of
persons who are alleged to be eyewitnesses shall also be duly
recorded and taken into consideration before submitting a report to
the Court of competent jurisdiction.
7. The SIT shall also examine the version of the other side and
shall complete the investigation dispassionately.  It is directed
that the investigation shall be completed within two months.
3

--- Page 312 ---
Further  consequences  shall  depend  upon  the  outcome  of  the
investigation.
8. It is clarified that we have not expressed any opinion on the
merits of the allegations or counter-allegations.
9. The  Writ  Petitions  stand  disposed  of  with  pending
application(s), if any.
(NITIN TALREJA)                            (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        DY. REGISTRAR

Writ Petition(Civil)  No.153/2026:-
1. The petitioner before us has invoked the writ jurisdiction of
this Court under Article 32 of the Constitution and has prayed for

--- Page 314 ---
2
the following reliefs:-
“(a) Issue of a writ of certiorari or any other appropriate writ
to quash the Show Cause Notice No. 1204/2025-26 dated September
30,  2025  issued  by  Respondent  No.  2  to  the  Petitioner.
(b) Pass an order of ad-interim stay, staying the proceedings
initiated through Impugned SCN, viz. No. 1204/2025-26 dated
September 30, 2025, issued by the Respondent No. 2 and direct
Respondent No. 3 to not pass an order before the issues referred
in instant petition are decided and refrain from adjudicating
the Impugned SCN pending disposal of this writ petition, and
thereby render justice.
(c) Pass any such other order granting relief in favour of the
Petitioner and against the Respondents, which this Hon'ble Court
may deem fit in the facts and circumstances of the case.”
2. In  view  of  the  recent  pronouncement  of  this  Court  in
“DIRECTORATE GENERAL OF GOODS AND SERVICES TAX INTELLIGENCE (HQS) &
ORS. VERSUS GAMESKRAFT TECHNOLOGIES PRIVATE LIMITED AND ORS.” etc.
(Civil Appeal Nos.8241–8244 of 2026 etc.) dated 27-5-2026, we need
not now adjudicate this petition on merits. 
3. The petitioner will have to appear before the Assessing
Officer and ultimately if any final order of assessment is passed
and the petitioner is aggrieved by the same, they may avail the
remedy of Statutory Appeal provided under the Act. 
4. With the aforesaid, this petition stands disposed of.
5. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.
W.P.(C) No. 174/2026:-
1. The petitioner has invoked the writ jurisdiction of this Court
under Article 32 of the Constitution and has prayed for the
following reliefs:-
“a)  Issue  a  writ,  order  or  direction  in  the  nature  of  
certiorari  quashing and setting aside the Impugned SCN dated  
31.07.2024 issued by Respondent No. 3;
b) Declare  the  entire  proceedings  initiated by Respondent No.3 
against  the  Petitioner  culminating  in  the  Impugned SCN dated 
31.07.2024, as contrary to law and quash and set aside the same;
c) Alternatively, issue a writ, order or direction in the nature of 
mandamus  prohibiting  Respondents,  their agents and servants from 
initiating any  recovery  proceedings  with  respect to the Impugned
SCN;

--- Page 315 ---
3
d) Hold that Rule 31A(3) of the GST Rules is ultra vires Article 246
and  Article  366(12A) as well as section 9 read with Section 15 and
Section 7 of GST Act;
e) Hold  Notification  No. 3/2018 - Central Tax dated 23.01.2018 and
Notification No. 30/ST-2 dated 25.01.2018 is ultra vires  Section 15
and Section 164 of the GST Act
f) Issue  such  other  appropriate writ, order or directions as this
Hon'ble Court may deem just and proper to issue in the circumstances
of the case.
2. Dr. S. Muralidhar, the learned senior counsel appearing for
the petitioner would submit that despite there being an interim
order passed by this Court that no coercive steps shall be taken
against the petitioner, the Assessing Officer proceeded with the
hearing of the impugned show cause notice and passed the final
order of assessment determining the tax liability of the petitioner
to the tune of Rs.6.3 Crore.
3. We are of the view that since final order of assessment has
already been passed, the petitioner may now prefer an appeal as
provided under the Act.
4. It shall also be open for the petitioner to raise all
contentions available to them in law before the appellate authority
including the issue of revaluation and pre-deposit.
5. We grant four weeks time to the petitioner to prefer an
appropriate Statutory Appeal against the final order of assessment.
6. With  the  aforesaid,  this  petition  stands  disposed  of.
7. Pending applications, if any, also stand disposed of.

SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CIVIL) Diary No. 36292/2026
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated 17-12-2025
in EA No. 220/2023 passed by the National Consumers Disputes
Redressal Commission, New Delhi]
M/S PRATEEK REALTORS INDIA PVT. LTD.               Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
ANEESH CHAND MATHUR & ORS.                         Respondent(s)
IA No. 188549/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN FILING
IA No. 188551/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
 
Date : 13-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE UJJAL BHUYAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ATUL S. CHANDURKAR
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Sudeep Singh, Adv.
                    Mr. Sameer Singh, Adv.
                    Ms. Neelam Singh, AOR
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Considering the order which we propose to pass, issuance of
notice to the respondents is considered not necessary.
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that an amount of
7,58,235.00 (seven lakh fifty Eight thousand two hundred and₹
thirty  five  rupees  only)  has  been  paid  by  the  petitioner
(developer)  towards  subvention  charges  on  behalf  of  the
respondents-flat buyers. Therefore, the said amount is required to
1

--- Page 317 ---
be  deducted  while  refunding  the  amount  deposited  by  the
respondents-flat buyers.
3. We find that execution proceedings are pending and the next
date  is  15.07.2026.  The  National  Consumer  Disputes  Redressal
Commission (Commission) has already clarified in its order dated
10.06.2026 that any dispute in calculation with respect to balance
as recorded in the order dated 08.04.2026 shall be taken up on
15.07.2026. Order dated 08.04.2026 is as under:
“1.In compliance of earlier orders, the JD
has  placed  before  us  a  copy  of  their
calculation, a copy of which is yet to be
supplied to the decree holders. As per this
calculation, the JD admits the balance amount
payable to be Rs.7.91 lacs only, and not
Rs.15.63  lacs  as  claimed  by  the  decree
holders. The counsel for the JD undertakes to
pay this amount within a maximum of two
weeks. Accordingly, the following orders are
passed.
(i)  The JD is hereby directed to pay
the undisputed and admitted amount of
Rs.7.91 lacs to the decree holders
within a maximum of one week from
today, positively.
(ii)  Supply  the  copy  of  their
calculation  to  the  decree  holders
within  three  days  from  today.  The
decree holders will have another one
week  to  verify  these  calculations.
Dispute  in  calculations  will  be
looked into on the next date.
2.  List the matter on 29.04.2026”
4. This being the position, we are of the view that it would be
open to the petitioner to raise the aforesaid ground before the
executing court on 15.07.2026 when the execution proceedings are
taken up or on subsequent dates thereafter. Till the issue is
2

--- Page 318 ---
finally  decided  by  the  Commission,  the  aforesaid  amount  of
7,58,235.00 (seven lakh fifty Eight thousand two hundred and₹
thirty five rupees only) already deposited by the petitioner with
the Commission shall not be released in favour of the respondents-
flat buyers.
5. This disposes of the Special Leave Petition.
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
(IRAMNAZ)                                       (CHETNA BALOONI)
COURT MASTER (SH)                               COURT MASTER (NSH)

and perused the material placed on record.
Considering  the  facts  and
circumstances of the case, the period of
incarceration undergone by the petitioner,
i.e. almost 7 years, and the fact that the
co-accused has been granted bail, we  are
inclined to grant bail to the petitioner.

circumstances of the case, we direct that
the petitioner be released on bail on such
terms and conditions as may be imposed by
the Trial Court in connection with Case
Crime No.124 of 2019 registered at Police
Station  Doiwala,  District  Dehradun,
Uttarakhand.

be open for them to approach this Court
for recall of this order.

pending  application(s)  are  disposed  of
accordingly.

PRIYA SOLANKI                           PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
PUSHPENDRA SINGH NATHAVAT              RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. This transfer petition has been filed by the
petitioner seeking transfer of Divorce Petition bearing
Family Main Case No.172 of 2024 titled “Priya Solanki
vs.  Pushpendra  Singh  Nathavat” from  the  Principal
Judge, Family Court, Bundi, Rajasthan to the Principal
Judge, Family Court, Bangalore, Karnataka.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties and perused
the material placed on record.
3. We have considered the facts of the case and
grounds on which transfer has been sought.  On such
consideration, we are satisfied with the grounds urged
by the petitioner and accept the same. Therefore, the
case bearing Family Main Case No.172 of 2024  titled
“Priya Solanki vs. Pushpendra Singh Nathavat”  pending
before the Family Court, Bundi, Rajasthan is ordered to
be  transferred  to  the  Family  Court,  Bangalore,
Karnataka.
1

--- Page 335 ---
4. Records  of  the  case  should  immediately  be
transferred to the transferee court.
5. The  parties  may  explore  the  possibility  of
settlement  through  Mediation.  The  transferee  court
would obtain the desire and wishes of the parties and
proceed accordingly in this regard.
6. The parties are at liberty initially to appear
through virtual mode. If such request is made, the
concerned court may favourably consider the same and
thereafter, if need be, the parties can be asked to
appear through physical mode.
7. The Transfer Petition is allowed accordingly.
8. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

Mr. Anil Kaushik, learned Additional Solicitor
General, upon instructions, states that the Rules under
the Bhartiya Vayuyan Adhiniyam 2024, have already been
framed  and  at  present  are  in  the  process  of
translation. He further submits that the same need to
be placed before the both Houses of Parliament for a
period  of  thirty  days,  which  submission  has  been
objected by Mr. Ravindra Srivastava, learned senior
counsel appearing for the petitioner, stating that the
Rules are first to be published and thereafter they
have to be placed before the Parliament. As such, the
respondents need to produce the Rules before this Court
without awaiting placement of the same before the both
Houses of Parliament.
Considering the aforesaid submission, we grant
two weeks’ time to the respondents to place before this
W.P.(C)No.1124/2025

Court the Rules, in a sealed cover, which have been
framed irrespective of the fact whether they are placed
before the Houses of Parliament or not.

S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Transfer Petition(s)(Civil) No(s).3113/2024
SHRIMATI GANGA PANDEY                              Petitioner(s)
VERSUS
ANIL KUMAR PANDEY AND ANR.                         Respondent(s)
[PART HEARD BY: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA AND HON'BLE MR. 
JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN]
IA No. 267003/2024 - EX-PARTE STAY
IA No. 267004/2024 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
Date : 13-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing today.
CORAM :
HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
For Petitioner(s) : Mr. Sukesh Ghosh, Adv.
Mr. Rajib Mishra, Adv.
Mr. P.C. Das, Adv.
Mr. Ashis Kumar Chowdhury, Adv.
Mr. Prathvi Raj Chauhan, AOR
Mr. Nawab Sidique, Adv.
For Respondent(s) : Mr. Subhasish Bhowmick, Adv.
Mr. Pramod Kumar Singh, Adv.
Mr. Suraj Kumar Singh, Adv.
Ms. Sundri, Adv.
Ms. Nisha Rani, Adv.
Ms. Padmavathi N, Adv.
Mr. Parminder Singh Bhullar, AOR
UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
O R D E R
1. We have heard this matter substantially over a period of
time. In fact, we started hearing it from the year 2024. Our
order dated 28.08.2025 is important. The same reads thus:-
1

--- Page 341 ---
T.P.(C) No.3113/2024
“1. Today, in the course of hearing of this matter,
we have been able to persuade the respondent –
husband to put an end to this long-drawn litigation
with  his  wife  by  depositing  an  amount  of
Rs.25,00,000/- (Rupees Twenty Five Lakh only) with
the Registry of this Court within a period of eight
weeks from today.
2. Although the wife is very reluctant to accept
this amount, we believe that it will be in her own
interest more particularly the daughter.
3. To accept this amount and put an end to this
entire  dispute  between  the  parties.  It  is
ultimately upto the wife to accept this amount.
4. For the present, we direct the husband to
deposit  the  amount  of  Rs.25,00,000/-  with  the
Registry of this Court within a period of eight
weeks from today.
5. Post this matter after eight weeks.”
2. It  is  not  in  dispute  that  the  respondent  deposited
Rs.25,00,000/- with the Registry of this Court, as directed by
this Court. Out of Rs.25,00,000/- deposited by the respondent,
we permitted the petitioners, i.e., the wife and the daughter
of  the  respondent  to  withdraw  Rs.8,00,000/-  for  their
maintenance and welfare. The total amount now left with the
Registry is Rs.17,00,000/-.
3. For all this period of time, we were persuading the
respondent  to  pay  something  to  his  daughter.  We  kept
persuading him to consider on humanitarian grounds that his
daughter is now in college, she is major and is completely
dependent on her maternal uncle. The mother is also helpless.
She has no other means of income. However, the respondent kept
on telling us that he has remarried and has two children born
2

--- Page 342 ---
T.P.(C) No.3113/2024
in the second wedlock and to be looked after.
4. We are of the view that we should now close this matter.
5. We direct the respondent to deposit a further amount of
Rs.5,00,000/- with the Registry of this Court within a period
of four weeks from today. Once this amount of Rs.5,00,000/- is
deposited, the total amount will be Rs.22,00,000/-, excluding
the  amount  of  Rs.8,00,000/-,  which  the  petitioners  have
already withdrawn.
6. We  shall  pass  final  order  of  disbursement  of
Rs.22,00,000/- in favour of the petitioners once the amount of
Rs.5,00,000/- is deposited by the respondent with the Registry
within a period of four weeks from today.
7. Post this matter on 17.08.2026.
(HARPREET KAUR)                                (POOJA SHARMA) 
COURT MASTER (SH)                             COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

--- Page 343 ---
1

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
 CIVIL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION   
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO. 1980 OF 2026
IN
TRANSFER PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 790 OF 2026
MONIKA RANJAN MALPANI   APPLICANT/
PETITIONER(S)
                                VERSUS
RANJAN MAHESH MALPANI      RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R 
1. The applicant/petitioner has moved the instant miscellaneous
application for modification of the order dated 14.05.2016 passed
in Transfer Petition (C) No. 799 of 2026 whereby this Court  while
allowing the Transfer Petition, directed transfer of case bearing
F. Suit No. 26 of 2026 titled as “Ranjan Mahesh Malpani vs. Monika
Ranjan Malpani ” pending  before the Family Court, Vapi, Valsad,
Gujarat to  the  Court  of  competent  jurisdiction  at  Jodhpur,
Rajasthan. 
2. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that the applicant
has inadvertently mentioned that the case be transferred to the
Court of competent jurisdiction at Jodhpur, Rajasthan whereas the
applicant is permanent resident of Phalodi, Rajasthan and other two
cases instituted by the applicant are also pending consideration
1 

--- Page 347 ---
before the Court at Phalodi, Rajasthan. Learned counsel for the
applicant prays that aforesaid case may also be transferred to
Phalodi, Rajasthan instead of Court of competent jurisdiction at
Jodhpur, Rajasthan. 
3. Heard learned counsel for the applicant.
4. The applicant may approach the High Court for transfer of case
from the Court of competent jurisdiction at Jodhpur to the Family
Court, Phalodi, Rajasthan. The High Court may consider the matter
on its own merits and pass appropriate orders after hearing both
the parties.
5. With this clarification, the Miscellaneous Application stands
disposed of.

   [PAMIDIGHANTAM SRI NARASIMHA]
     …………………………………………………………………………J.
   [ALOK ARADHE]

SLP (Crl.) D. No.34992/2026 1

--- Page 356 ---
         UPON hearing the counsel the Court made 
the following
                      O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The  petitioner  who  has  been  in  judicial
custody  for  more  than  one  year  on  a  crime
registered under Sections 20(B) and 29 of the
Narcotics Drug And Psychotropic Substances Act,
1995 seeks regular bail. The FIR No. 128 of 2025
produced  at  Annexure  P1  is  dated  14.05.2025.
Allegedly, on a secret information received, two
cars were detained upon which three occupants fled
into the forest and the petitioner herein was
apprehended.  Total  of  61  Kgs  of  Ganja was
recovered out of which 29 Kgs was recovered from
one car and 32 Kgs from the other. The bail
applications filed before the Sessions Judge and
the High Court were rejected.
3. The petitioner has been in custody and has
also been interrogated according to the police.
The petitioner’s contention is that he was unaware
SLP (Crl.) D. No.34992/2026 2

--- Page 357 ---
of the Ganja being carried in the vehicle and that
he has no criminal antecedents.
4. As of now, the trial has commenced and only
three  out  of  thirty  one  witnesses  have  been
examined.  On  the  totality  of  the  facts  and
circumstances, we are of the opinion that the
petitioner can be released on bail, pending trial
subject to terms and conditions as imposed by the
trial court.
5. With  the  aforesaid,  this  petition  stands
disposed of.
6. Pending  applications,  if  any,  also  stand
disposed of.
(HARPREET KAUR)                  (POOJA SHARMA)

IA No. 138679/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF

IA No. 138676/2026 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING O.T.
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for 
hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE J.B. PARDIWALA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K. VINOD CHANDRAN
For Appellant(s) : Mr. Rahul Jain, AOR
                   S. Muthukrishnan, Adv.
For Respondent(s) :Ms. Vrinda Bhandari, AOR
                   Ms. Pragya Barsaiyan, Adv.
                   Ms. Nitya Jain, Adv.
                   Ms. Vanshita Gupta, Adv.
                   Mr. Bharathimohan M., Adv.
                    
        UPON hearing the counsel the Court made 
the following
                      O R D E R
1. The appellant stood trial for offences under
Crl. Appeal No.2569/2026 1/3

--- Page 359 ---
Sections 366 and 367 of the Indian Penal Code,
1860 (IPC) and was convicted and sentenced to
undergo rigorous imprisonment for 10 years along
with  appropriate  fine  imposed  and  default
sentence. 
2. The  appeal  filed  by  the  appellant  was
rejected by the High Court. The appellant has been
in custody for almost four and a half years. 
3. We heard Mr. Rahul Jain, learned counsel
appearing  for  the  appellant  and  Ms.  Vrinda
Bhandari,  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondent.
4. Learned counsel for the appellant points out
certain  discrepancies  in  the  evidence,  which
according to him, would eventually result in an
acquittal. It is also pointed out that the mother
who was examined as PW-1, in whose company the
victim was when she was alleged to have been
abducted by the accused, had spoken contrary to
the statement before the police. The learned State
counsel, however, would urge that the victim was a
Crl. Appeal No.2569/2026 2/3

--- Page 360 ---
mentally challenged girl and both the courts have
concurred on the aspect of conviction. 
5. On a prima facie consideration, we are of the
opinion that this is a good case for suspending
the sentence and releasing the appellant on bail,
pending final disposal of the appeal.
6. The sentence imposed on the appellant is
suspended and he is ordered to be released on
bail, subject to terms and conditions that the
trial court may deem fit and proper to impose.
7. Registry  shall  call  for  the  records  and
proceedings of the trial court in both forms,
physical and digital. The digital record shall be
provided  to  both  the  learned  counsel  at  the
earliest.
8. Notify the main appeal for final hearing in
February 2027. 
(HARPREET KAUR)                   (POOJA SHARMA)
COURT MASTER (SH)               COURT MASTER (NSH)

ITEM NO.2            COURT NO.14              SECTION II-B
             S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                   RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (Crl.) No.8548/2026
[Arising out of impugned judgment and order dated 27-11-
2025 in CRM (NDPS) No. 956/2025 passed by the High Court at
Calcutta]
SHUVO GHOSH                                  Petitioner(s)
                             VERSUS
UNION OF INDIA                               Respondent(s)
IA  No.  130080/2026  -  EXEMPTION  FROM  FILING  C/C  OF
I/JUDGMENT,  IA  No.130078/2026  -  PERMISSION  TO  FILE
ADDL.DOCS./FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 14-07-2026 This matter was called on for hearing 
today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE K.V. VISWANATHAN
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARUN PALLI
For Petitioner(s)  Mr. Rudrajit Ghosh. Adv.*
    Ms. Manjeet Kirpal, AOR
                   
For Respondent(s)  Mr. Anil Kaushik, A.S.G.
                   Ms. Shambhavi Sharma, Adv.
                   Mr. Akshat Agarwal, Adv.
                   Ms. Astha Singh, Adv.
                   Ms. Prerna Dhall, Adv.
                   Mr. Yogesh Vats, Adv.
                   Dr. N. Visakamurthy, AOR   
      UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                                   O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Rudrajit Ghosh, learned counsel for
the petitioner and Mr. Anil Kaushik, learned Additional
Solicitor General appearing for the Union of India.
2. The  present  petition  calls  in  question  the

--- Page 362 ---
2
correctness of the order dated 27-11-2025 in CRM (NDPS)
No. 956/2025 passed by the High Court at Calcutta. By
the said order, the petitioner has been denied the
benefit  of  bail  in  connection  with  NCB  Crime
No.15/NCB/KOL/2024  (NDPS  Case  No.N-77/2024)  under
Sections 8(c), 21(c), 25, 27A & 29 of the Narcotic
Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 (for short,
‘NDPS’).
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that
the case of the prosecution is that the contraband was
recovered allegedly from the conscious possession of
the accused- Dharmendra Singh @ Dharmendra Pal Singh
(co-accused). According to the learned counsel for the
petitioner, the petitioner herein- Shuvo Ghosh and
Kousik Haldar (co-accused) were brought in the case, on
the statement of Dharmendra Singh @ Dharmendra Pal
Singh.  Learned  counsel  further  submits  that  the
petitioner  has  been  in  custody  since  06.09.2024.
Learned counsel also submits that Kousik Haldar, who is
identically  situated,  has  been  enlarged  on  bail.
Learned counsel draws attention to paragraph 7 of the
counter-affidavit filed by the Union of India (UOI)
where the UOI has mentioned that kingpin of the entire
episode was Gautam Mondal. Learned counsel submits that
Gautam Mondal has been enlarged on bail.
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties
and considered the submissions made by them. We have

--- Page 363 ---
3
also kept in mind the principles enshrined in Section
37 of the NDPS. Considering that Kousik Haldar who is
identically situated is on bail and Gautam Mondal who
is a kingpin according to the prosecution, is also on
bail and those bail orders have attained finality, we
are of the opinion that the ingredients of Section 37
of NDPS are satisfied in favour of the petitioner-
Shuvo Ghosh.
5. Accordingly, we  direct that  the petitioner-
Shuvo  Ghosh be  enlarged  on  bail  su bject  to  the
satisfaction of the concerned Trial Court in connection
with the aforesaid NCB Crime case and for the offences
mentioned hereinabove.
6. The Special Leave Petition is disposed of in the
above terms.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.
(NIRMALA NEGI)                             (MANOJ KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                  COURT MASTER (NSH)
*appearance not given

FOR ADMISSION
IA No. 191173/2026 - CONDONATION OF DELAY IN REFILING /  CURING THE
DEFECTS
IA No. 191174/2026 - STAY APPLICATION
 
Date : 13-07-2026 These matters were called on for hearing today.
CORAM : 
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE MANOJ MISRA
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE VIJAY BISHNOI
For Petitioner(s) : 
                    Krishnamohan K., AOR
                   Mr. Balbir Singh,, Sr. Adv.
1

--- Page 365 ---
                   Mr. Krishnamohan K, Adv.
                   Mr. Bhishma Ahluwalia, Adv.
                   Ms. Prerna Jain Kala, Adv.
                   Ms. Shivali Shah, Adv.
                   Ms. Pragati Tiwari, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) : 
          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1. Delay condoned.
2. The issue that arises for our consideration in these
appeals  is:  “whether  washing  of  ore  to  remove  its
impurities would amount to treating it as to make it a
concentrate?”
3. The  submission  of  the  learned  counsel  for  the
appellant(s)  is  that  on  identical  issue  earlier  the
Tribunal had treated the imported goods as an ore and not
a concentrate; whereas, by the impugned order, the same
has been treated as a concentrate by placing reliance on
explanatory  notes  of  HSN  (Harmonized  System  of
Nomenclature).  It  is  submitted  that  even  in  the
explanatory note in Chapter 26 of HSN, it is stated that
for the purposes of headings 26.01 to 26.17, the term
‘concentrate’ applies to ores which have had part or all
of the foreign matter removed by special treatments,
either because such foreign matter might hamper subsequent
metallurgical operations or with a view to economical
2

--- Page 366 ---
transport. It is the case of the appellant(s) that there
is no evidence in respect of those special treatments and,
therefore, the import ought to have been accepted as an
ore.
4. Issue notice, returnable in eight weeks.
5. In the meantime, the interim order, if any, which the
appellant(s) were enjoying during the pendency of the
proceedings before the Tribunal, shall continue.
(CHETAN ARORA)                                  (SAPNA BANSAL)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        COURT MASTER (NSH)
3

DX-4655 and the allegation against the petitioner(s) is
that they were piloting the vehicle. It has also been
submitted that there are no criminal antecedents and has
been falsely implicated in the crime. The trial will take a
long time and some of the co-accused persons have been
granted bail.
3. On  the  other  hand,  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent-State, has vehemently opposed the application
for grant of bail.
4. This court, after hearing learned counsel for the
parties and after going through the record, is of the
opinion that the application for grant of bail deserves to
be allowed and is accordingly allowed.

enlarged on bail in connection with FIR No. 65 of 2025,

Bail to the petitioner for a period of three months.
2. It has been brought to the notice of this Court that
the order granting interim bail has been further extended
till 07.08.2026.

granting interim bail for three months and no cogent reason
has been assigned while granting interim bail and the
interim bail has already been extended for a period of
three months. Resultantly, the High Court is requested to
decide the bail petition finally, instead of granting
interim bail in the matter.

concluded as early as possibly, preferably within a period
of two months from today.
5. The Trial Court shall also be free to decide the
matter finally at an early date.
6. The observation made by the High Court in the order
granting interim bail, will not come in the way of the
Trial Court while deciding the matter finally, on merits.

Petition stands disposed of.
